ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general nigiri sushi class (FOODON_00002694); no specific salmon-mentaiko nigiri subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers nigiri sushi generically; no mentaiko-specific variant code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for salmon nigiri with mentaiko identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One piece of salmon nigiri with mentaiko weighs approximately 35 g, comprising roughly 20 g seasoned sushi rice, 10 g fresh salmon slice, and 5 g mentaiko topping.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon Nigiri (Mentaiko) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05100123 is sourced directly from HPB Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are preserved from the HPB source. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite (salmon + sushi rice + pollock roe) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for salmon nigiri with mentaiko exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is assigned based on the GI of white sushi rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Sushi rice is the dominant carbohydrate contributor (22.66 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Salmon and mentaiko contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (72 × 7.93) / 100 ≈ 5.7, based on 35 g serving size providing approximately 7.93 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from salt-cured mentaiko (pollock roe) and seasoned sushi rice
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 336 mg per 100 g (approximately 118 mg per piece) is elevated; individuals with hypertension should limit to 1–2 pieces per meal and avoid additional soy sauce dipping to keep cumulative sodium within the 2,000 mg daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI sushi rice with low dietary fibre drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sushi rice has a GI of approximately 72 and provides 22.66 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.23 g dietary fibre, resulting in a rapid glycaemic response; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces, pair with high-fibre accompaniments such as edamame or seaweed salad, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon offset partially by high sodium from mentaiko
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon provides cardioprotective EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids and estimated saturated fat is low at approximately 1.8 g per 100 g; however, the high sodium content (336 mg per 100 g) from mentaiko is a concern for blood pressure management in cardiovascular disease, and portion should be limited to 2–3 pieces with no added soy sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 from salmon beneficial; high sodium and potential nightshade content from chilli in mentaiko are cautions
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Salmon and pollock roe provide EPA/DHA omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory benefit for rheumatoid arthritis; however, the high sodium (336 mg per 100 g) may worsen tissue swelling and chilli in mentaiko (a nightshade) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals — limit to 2 pieces per meal and monitor individual tolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon nigiri with mentaiko is a Japanese sushi preparation consisting of a hand-formed oval of seasoned sushi rice topped with a slice of fresh raw salmon and a dollop of mentaiko, which is salt-cured and typically spiced pollock roe (tarako). The sushi rice is cooked short-grain Japanese rice seasoned with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, giving it a slightly sweet and tangy flavour. The salmon topping is soft, buttery, and rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), while the mentaiko adds a briny, umami-forward, mildly spicy flavour and a slightly granular texture. Each piece weighs approximately 35 g. Nutritionally, per 100 g this dish provides 200 kcal, 10.38 g protein, 7.57 g fat, and 22.66 g carbohydrate with 1.23 g dietary fibre and 336 mg sodium. The high sodium content is primarily attributable to the salt-curing process used for mentaiko. The GI is approximately 72 (high), driven by the white sushi rice base. Clinically, this dish is not recommended for individuals with hypertension or type 2 diabetes in large portions due to high sodium and high-GI carbohydrate content respectively. The omega-3 content from salmon is beneficial for cardiovascular health and may have anti-inflammatory effects relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. This dish is commonly served in Japanese sushi restaurants and conveyor-belt sushi (kaiten-zushi) establishments across Singapore and Japan.
common_user_queries: Is salmon nigiri with mentaiko healthy?
How many calories in salmon mentaiko nigiri?
Is mentaiko nigiri high in sodium?
Is salmon nigiri good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo canonical record (cr_id F05100123, confidence 0.95, provenance canonical, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite as HPB does not report this breakdown; all other primary macros and micronutrients are HPB-sourced. GI is an analogous estimate based on white sushi rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for a composite of cooked short-grain white rice, raw Atlantic salmon, and pollock roe, as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. This is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific salmon-mentaiko nigiri preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish as of 2026. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white sushi rice (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for salmon nigiri with mentaiko exists in approved sources. 4. Halal status is marked false as mentaiko (pollock roe) preparation and certification status is not confirmed for generic preparations. 5. The rheumatoid arthritis caution regarding nightshades applies specifically when mentaiko contains chilli; standard mentaiko preparations do include chilli pepper.
